title: SMS/Telegram > Meshcore Bridge + More - Testers wanted

messages.author: basiccode12
messages.text: Looking for testers for the below. Please provide feedback to the support section in the 3 dots of the app. ANDROID ONLY MeshCore Communication Bridge What It Does: Bridges your MeshCore device (e.g., Lilygo T-Deck) to internet services like Telegram, SMS, and APIs. Works off-grid via LoRa mesh and Bluetooth. Getting Started: 1. Install the app on Android 12+. 2. Connect to your "bridging" MeshCore device via Bluetooth. 3. Configure Services in Settings: Telegram: API ID, Hash, Phone Number. SMS API (optional): Twilio/Vonage keys. 4. Add the channels to your second "roaming" device (eg Lillygo T-Deck) Handles: Handles are auto-generated from contacts: Example: John Smith → @johnsmith Use handles to send messages: @mom Call me when you can @johnsmith Hi there! Unknown numbers use full number: @15551234567 Basic Commands: #help → Show available commands #sync → Sync messages since last activity #syncn N → Sync last N messages (max 10) #synch H → Sync last H hours (max 24) Channels: Telegram → Bridges Telegram messages. SMS → Bridges phone SMS. SMS (API) → Bridges via Twilio/Vonage/etc. Trading (crypto/forex) Weather Groq AI (AI responses) Quick Tips: You must receive a message first to create a handle. After using a handle, you can continue chatting without repeating it. Example Conversation: ← SMS from Mom: "Are you coming for dinner?" → Bridge creates @mom ↓ You see on MeshCore: "@mom Are you coming for dinner?" → You type: "@mom Yes, be there at 6" → Bridge sends SMS to Mom ← Mom replies: "Great, see you then!" ↓ You see: "@mom Great, see you then!" → You type: "Should I bring anything?" → No handle needed! Bridge remembers last contact ← Mom replies: "Just yourself " ↓ You see: "@mom Just yourself " Key Points: First message creates the handle (@mom). After that, you only need the handle when switching contacts. The bridge remembers the last active contact for follow-ups.
messages.content_html:
Looking for testers for the below. Please provide feedback to the support section in the 3 dots of the app. ANDROID ONLY MeshCore Communication Bridge What It Does:
  • Bridges your MeshCore device (e.g., Lilygo T-Deck) to internet services like Telegram, SMS, and APIs.
  • Works off-grid via LoRa mesh and Bluetooth. Getting Started:
1. Install the app on Android 12+. 2. Connect to your "bridging" MeshCore device via Bluetooth. 3. Configure Services in Settings:
  • Telegram: API ID, Hash, Phone Number.
  • SMS API (optional): Twilio/Vonage keys.
4. Add the channels to your second "roaming" device (eg Lillygo T-Deck) Handles:
  • Handles are auto-generated from contacts: Example: John Smith → @johnsmith
  • Use handles to send messages: @mom Call me when you can @johnsmith Hi there!
  • Unknown numbers use full number: @15551234567 Basic Commands:
#help → Show available commands #sync → Sync messages since last activity #syncn N → Sync last N messages (max 10) #synch H → Sync last H hours (max 24) Channels:
  • Telegram → Bridges Telegram messages.
  • SMS → Bridges phone SMS.
  • SMS (API) → Bridges via Twilio/Vonage/etc.
  • Trading (crypto/forex)
  • Weather
  • Groq AI (AI responses) Quick Tips:
  • You must receive a message first to create a handle.
  • After using a handle, you can continue chatting without repeating it.
Example Conversation: ← SMS from Mom: "Are you coming for dinner?" → Bridge creates @mom ↓ You see on MeshCore: "@mom Are you coming for dinner?" → You type: "@mom Yes, be there at 6" → Bridge sends SMS to Mom ← Mom replies: "Great, see you then!" ↓ You see: "@mom Great, see you then!" → You type: "Should I bring anything?" → No handle needed! Bridge remembers last contact ← Mom replies: "Just yourself " ↓ You see: "@mom Just yourself " Key Points:
  • First message creates the handle (@mom).
  • After that, you only need the handle when switching contacts.
  • The bridge remembers the last active contact for follow-ups.

messages.text: Mmm I dunno man. You say Google will report it as malware. Telegram is compromised (RU). SMS is compromised (Salt Typhoon). Grok is compromised (US/politics). Should this really be added?

messages.author: basiccode12
messages.text: "Trojan-SMS.AndroidOS.FakeInst" is a false positive. Google flags apps that request SMS permissions and match certain patterns. Your app needs SMS for bridging, which triggers this. Why this happens FakeInst trojans typically: Request SMS permissions Send premium-rate SMS without consent Read SMS messages Have minimal/no user-facing functionality Your app legitimately needs SMS for bridging, but the automated scanner flags it based on heuristics.
messages.content_html: "Trojan-SMS.AndroidOS.FakeInst" is a false positive. Google flags apps that request SMS permissions and match certain patterns. Your app needs SMS for bridging, which triggers this. Why this happens FakeInst trojans typically: Request SMS permissions Send premium-rate SMS without consent Read SMS messages Have minimal/no user-facing functionality Your app legitimately needs SMS for bridging, but the automated scanner flags it based on heuristics.

messages.author: Beanow
messages.text: I use none of those APIs, and this is part of the reason. But this could be like paying for a private email host on your side, to send it to a gmail user. They'll leak it for you if you're not careful. That said, I'm not claiming this is malware, I'm just sharing the report as-is without my analysis on it. And I'm also not paranoid enough to say we should all abandon phones and internet services. (Or I wouldn't be on Discord). There's probably a place for it for some people but I don't have a need personally.
messages.content_html: I use none of those APIs, and this is part of the reason. But this could be like paying for a private email host on your side, to send it to a gmail user. They'll leak it for you if you're not careful. That said, I'm not claiming this is malware, I'm just sharing the report as-is without my analysis on it. And I'm also not paranoid enough to say we should all abandon phones and internet services. (Or I wouldn't be on Discord). There's probably a place for it for some people but I don't have a need personally.

messages.author: basiccode12
messages.text: I kinda get you. But i dont see who is going to leak it when the "gmail side" you have in the above exmple is actually a meshcore node in this case. The message content is only ever between the source SMS/Telegram/Groq and meshcore. The content doesnt get sent to any other external api and they all have different channels to prevent cross contamination. (edited)
messages.content_html: I kinda get you. But i dont see who is going to leak it when the "gmail side" you have in the above exmple is actually a meshcore node in this case. The message content is only ever between the source SMS/Telegram/Groq and meshcore. The content doesnt get sent to any other external api and they all have different channels to prevent cross contamination. (edited)
messages.reply_html:
Beanow
I use none of those APIs, and this is part of the reason. But this could be like paying for a private email host on your side, to send it to a gmail user. They'll leak it for you if you're not careful. That said, I'm not claiming this is malware, I'm just sharing the report as-is without my analysis on it. And I'm also not paranoid enough to say we should all abandon phones and internet services. (Or I wouldn't be on Discord). There's probably a place for it for some people but I don't have a need personally.

messages.author: basiccode12
messages.text: This is probably going to be asked so ill put it here. Exponential Backoff Throttling Messages sent to MeshCore use exponential backoff delays: Message # Delay Before Sending 1st 0ms (immediate) 2nd 100ms 3rd 200ms 4th 400ms 5th 800ms 6th 1600ms 7th+ 2000ms (capped) Resets after 30 sec of inactivity Flood Detection If messages from the same bridge (SMS, Telegram, or SMS API) are sent continuously for 30 seconds: Sending stops to prevent mesh congestion User is notified with a warning message Automatically resumes after 30 seconds of inactivity
messages.content_html: This is probably going to be asked so ill put it here. Exponential Backoff Throttling Messages sent to MeshCore use exponential backoff delays: Message # Delay Before Sending 1st 0ms (immediate) 2nd 100ms 3rd 200ms 4th 400ms 5th 800ms 6th 1600ms 7th+ 2000ms (capped) Resets after 30 sec of inactivity Flood Detection If messages from the same bridge (SMS, Telegram, or SMS API) are sent continuously for 30 seconds: Sending stops to prevent mesh congestion User is notified with a warning message Automatically resumes after 30 seconds of inactivity

messages.author: focaldistorsion
messages.text: if it's a niche tool then you're better off creating a fremium model of sort with some 3rd party auditing to gain credibility. you can't build a closed source product and expect people to buy. especially with SMS permissions which can covertly send SMSs to premium phone nos which charge the subscriber (a risk i just thought of) (edited)
